There are places in our relationships for both rewards and gifts. We're not trying to convince you to use only gifts. The most effective persuaders think about the nature of the relationship they want and then decide if gifts or rewards are more appropriate. Sleuths take this approach to the relationships they're trying to create. For example, a very successful orthodontist thought he was already using reciprocity powerfully in his waiting room. As patients entered his practice, he had his staff point to the refrigerator and say, please take a refreshment. It's our gift to you. But was he actually using the principle of reciprocity powerfully? Take a moment to think about why this was or wasn't a powerful use of reciprocity. It was free water, but it wasn't a potent gift because it was always available to everyone. It was seen as a consequence as an entitlement. Entitlements are perks or amenities that we come to expect, nothing out of the ordinary. How could the orthodontist change the perception of the water from an entitlement to a nice gift? One way would be to put the refrigerator behind the counter and instruct his staff to ask, could I offer you a refreshment? For patients who said yes, the receptionist could ask what type of water they would like, such as flavored, sparkling, or flat.
